titleStmt.title: Letter from William Upton Tyrrell to Miss Miller 29, June, 1917
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: William Upton Tyrrell

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p: William Upton Tyrrell describes his situation in France and wishes Miss Miller and her family well.

msIdentifier.repository: OPW-Maynooth University Archive & Research Centre at Castletown

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
29/6/17 My dear Miss Miller Just a line to tell you all is well
here and I hope all is well with you. It is quite a long time since I wrote to
you now as I have been rather busy
lately. Times have been somewhat lively
but now we are away back from the
line resting and enjoying ourselves
thoroughly. Get any amount of
strawberries etc. It is the best place I
have ever come across out here. I was not actually in the advance on the
7th as I was sent back as reserve. However I
went up a few days after and had a
most interesting exciting and occassionally
uncomfortable time. The last time in we
lived for 6 days in a lovely boche dug
out. The scenes are absolutely indescribable. The weather is very hot with frequent
heavy thunder storms. I do not think I am any where near
your brother yet. I know who he
is with I think; so I will know if I do
get any where near. I hope that by now your sister
has quite recovered from her illness. I do not know where you are now
but will address this to Salisbury. I get great letters from Minnie and
Bobby from school. They seem to be
having a great time and Bobby gets on
very well I think. I found the 1st Battalion the other day
and spent a very pleasant afternoon
with many old friends. All crops are very early out here.
They are working away at the hay
now. It makes things seem very
homelike. No news so will shut up with
best love from Yours very Sincerely
Will Tyrrell
